Member-only story

33 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you’ve spent any time solving DSA problems, you’ll soon find out that most of them always come back to arrays. Whether you’re just trying to get the hang of DSA problems in JavaScript, prepping for a technical interview, or just brushing up on the fundamentals, you’ll surely run into problems that tie back to arrays. They’re the core foundation of most DSA problems, so you need to get conversant with them early on is always a great idea.
TL;DR

• JavaScript arrays are dynamic and zero-indexed, which means they grow and shrink freely and the first element always sits at index 0, not index 1.

• Time complexity tells you how your code’s runtime grows as your input grows, while space complexity tells you how much extra memory it uses. The two you’ll see most are O(1), which stays constant, and O(n), which grows right along with your input.

• Array methods fall into three buckets, mutation methods that change the original array, non-mutation methods that hand you back a new one, and search methods that just look through what’s already there.

• In this guide, we’ll walk through all of this step by step, starting with how arrays actually work, then moving through the essential methods, the core DSA patterns, and the…
